ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general duck soup entry (FOODON_00002572); no specific code exists for the braised cabbage, old tofu, and vermicelli variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers meat-based soups broadly; no specific code for duck rack soup with tofu and vermicelli. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of this dish is approximately 500 g, comprising a generous bowl of broth with duck rack pieces, braised cabbage, old tofu, and vermicelli noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (NDB 05140); Cabbage, raw (NDB 11109); Firm tofu (NDB 16126); Mung bean noodles, cooked (NDB 20113); and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Chinese-style meat soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition: approximately 30% duck meat and skin (cooked), 20% napa cabbage (cooked), 20% old firm tofu, 20% cooked mung bean vermicelli, and 10% broth/water. Energy and macros calculated from USDA SR Legacy component values. Sodium estimated from typical Chinese braised soup preparation with soy sauce and salt, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA soup data. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-vegetable soups with noodles; analogous estimate based on mung bean vermicelli GI (GI=26–39) and low-GI vegetable/tofu components; University of Sydney GI Database reference for mung bean noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI estimated at 35 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being mung bean vermicelli (glass noodles), which the University of Sydney GI Database reports at GI 26–39 (mean ~33). Cabbage and tofu contribute negligible glycemic load. The composite dish GI is estimated at 35 (low). Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (35 × 24 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and braising broth
hypertension.guidance: Each 500 g serving provides approximately 1550 mg sodium, representing over 67% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and consume only a portion of the broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from mung bean vermicelli and high protein and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low estimated GI of 35 driven by mung bean glass noodles, and a moderate glycemic load of 8.4 per serving, making it a suitable choice for blood glucose management when portion size is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from duck skin and moderate sodium from braising liquid
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Duck skin contributes saturated fat (approximately 4.5 g per serving) which may raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove visible duck skin and limit broth intake to reduce sodium and saturated fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Duck Rack Braised Cabbage and Old Tofu Vermicelli (白菜豆腐粉条鸭汤) is a traditional Chinese-style braised soup dish served at Chinese restaurants in Singapore, notably at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant. The dish is built around the duck rack — the bony carcass or frame remaining after the breast and leg meat have been removed from a roasted duck — which is simmered slowly to extract a rich, flavourful broth. Napa cabbage (白菜) is braised in the broth until tender and sweet, old firm tofu (老豆腐) absorbs the savoury duck stock, and mung bean vermicelli (粉条, glass noodles) are added to provide a soft, slippery texture and mild starchy body. The soup is seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and salt, resulting in a clear to lightly golden broth with umami depth. Texturally, the dish offers contrasting elements: tender duck meat clinging to the rack bones, silky glass noodles, soft braised cabbage, and firm-yet-yielding tofu. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~72 kcal per 100 g), provides meaningful protein (~6.8 g per 100 g) from duck and tofu, and has a low glycemic index (~35) due to the mung bean vermicelli base. Sodium is elevated from soy sauce and broth, making portion control of the liquid important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for non-halal Chinese dining contexts. It is commonly ordered as a shared main dish or as a warming soup course in a multi-dish Chinese meal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for duck (cooked), firm tofu, mung bean vermicelli, and cabbage using a weighted-ingredient model. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean glass noodles. Confidence is medium.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for duck meat and skin (cooked, roasted), firm tofu, mung bean vermicelli (cooked), and napa cabbage (cooked), weighted at approximately 30/20/20/20/10 (duck/cabbage/tofu/vermicelli/broth). Sodium is estimated from typical Chinese braised soup preparation with soy sauce. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace in duck and plant-based components per USDA data). vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources for this composite dish. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. GI set to 35 as an analogous estimate based on mung bean glass noodles (University of Sydney GI Database, GI 26–39). Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (35 × 24 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 8.4. The dish is not halal-certified as duck preparation and soy sauce sourcing at the restaurant are unverified. FoodOn FOODON_00002572 and FoodEx2 A0FKP used as closest parent codes; no exact ontology code exists for this specific composite dish.
